data.title: If I Could Only Buy 3 REITs, It Would Be These
data.description:
I am often get asked questions like:

What REITs would you buy if you could buy just three of them?

And I get where this is coming from.

Most investors don't care enough about real estate investment trusts, or REITs (VNQ), to build a diversified portfolio of 15-20 names. Instead, they would rather own a handful of REITs to get their real estate exposure and then diversify across other sectors of the market.

That is perfectly understandable.

You should note, however, that if I could only invest in 3 REITs, my selection would be very different than if I could build a diversified portfolio of 15-20 REITs.

The reason why is that you cannot afford to take as much risk when you have so little diversification. To give you an example: in a portfolio of 20 REITs, I could include three small-cap, heavily discounted apartment REITs that present greater upside potential but are also more heavily leveraged. But if I could only own 3 REITs, then I would rather focus on one blue-chip apartment REIT with an investment-grade-rated balance sheet because I cannot afford to be wrong. If just one of my three picks disappoints, it would significantly harm my portfolio.

With that in mind, here are the 3 REITs that I would pick today.

All 3 are what I would describe as "blue-chip" REITs in that they own Class A assets, have strong investment-grade-rated balance sheets with low leverage, and their management teams enjoy exceptional track records. Despite that, they are exceptionally discounted today and offer investors a path to market-beating returns going forward.

Each REIT also focuses on a different property sector, which affords some level of diversification, even in this smaller portfolio.

EastGroup Properties, Inc. (EGP)

EastGroup Properties (EGP) would be my pick for industrial real estate.

There are three reasons why:

Firstly, it arguably owns the best portfolio in its peer group for the long run. These are modern Class A properties, mostly located in high-barrier-to-entry last-mile locations of rapidly growing Sunbelt markets, with Florida and Texas today representing 60% of its rental income.

EGP has itself developed most of these assets, and it has kept the best one for its own portfolio, focusing on the best locations and smaller shallow-bay properties, which are much more limited in supply but in constant growing demand:

This is resulting in solid rent growth even in today's tough environment.

Beyond the portfolio, EGP has one of the lowest levels of leverage in its peer group, with a very low 10% LTV. As a result, it is one of just a few REITs that has an A- credit rating. In comparison, many of its peers use about 2x more leverage, and even that's still conservative.

It is a great advantage as it reduces the impact of higher interest rates, and also positions the REIT for faster growth going forward, as it is in a position to increase leverage to take on new development projects, which should earn it significant spreads over its cost of capital, and expand its FFO per share.

Analysts are today expecting 8% average annual growth in FFO per share through 2030:

Finally, the last reason why I would favor EGP is its management, which is truly exceptional. Just look at their multi-decade track record since they went public, outperforming even the S&P 500 (SPY):

They have massively outperformed all major market indexes by following a relatively safe strategy, and I expect this long streak of outperformance to continue going forward, especially as the AI revolution takes hold, which should greatly increase the demand for its properties. You can read why by clicking here.

But despite all these qualities, the REIT is today trading at a 50% lower valuation than just a few years ago. Its AFFO multiple has dropped from 35x to just 17x. It is also considerably lower than its 5-year average multiple of 23x:

It is also priced at a 25% discount to our estimate of its NAV, which is quite exceptional for a REIT of this quality.

I think that as interest rates are cut down further and the narrative shifts to how the AI revolution will increase the demand for industrial properties, EGP and other industrial REITs will see their valuations expand closer to historic norms.

Simply recovering to its 5-year average could unlock 30% upside potential.

But even ignoring any potential upside from multiple expansion, the REIT is well-positioned to deliver double-digit annual total returns from its 8% annual growth and its 3.5% dividend yield going forward.

The risk-to-reward is very attractive in my opinion for these reasons.

Camden Property Trust (CPT)

Camden Property Trust would be my pick for residential real estate

I would favor it over its peers for similar reasons as EGP.

Firstly, its portfolio. It owns mostly affordable Class B apartment communities in growing Sunbelt markets. Its average rent is considerably lower than that of some close peers like Mid-America Communities (MAA). Such affordable properties are always in great demand, and may even increase in demand during recessions as people downgrade from Class A to Class B to reduce their expenses.

Its rent-to-income ratios are also low, which provides great margin of safety in case of a recession, and long-term growth potential as its markets keep enjoying strong population and job growth.

CPT is a developer just like EGP, and if it has decided to retain these properties for its own portfolio, it is because they stand out for their superior quality and long-term prospects:

Secondly, it also has one of the strongest balance sheets in the sector with an A- credit rating, which should give it ample capacity to keep developing new properties going forward. Historically, the REIT has been able to develop new properties at yields 100-200 basis points higher than market cap rates, creating a lot of value for shareholders.

Finally, its management also has an exceptional track record:

On their most recent earnings call, they noted that they expect rent growth to accelerate to 4% in 2026 and 5% in 2027, which, coupled with new development projects, should result in strong growth for the REIT.

Even then, the REIT is still trading at a steep 25% discount to our estimate of its NAV. What this essentially means is that it trades at a 6.2% implied cap rate when its assets are worth closer to 5% cap in the private market.

Simply returning to NAV has the potential to deliver 30% upside, and I think that it will get there as interest rates are cut and rent growth accelerates in 2026.

Big Yellow Group Plc (BYG / OTCPK:BYLOF)

Finally, I would invest in Big Yellow Group to diversify geographically.

Big Yellow is the leading self-storage REIT in Europe, and there is a simple reason why I have owned it for close to a decade now.

Self-storage has historically been the most rewarding REIT property sector in the U.S., earning near 20% average annual total returns:

It was so rewarding because self-storage was growing in demand, and this allowed REITs to develop new properties, earning 8-10% initial yields, resulting in unusually high spreads over their cost of capital.

Moreover, REITs enjoy very significant competitive advantages in this particular sector, due to their scale and professional management. They can develop valuable brands and implement national advertising campaigns on TV, billboards, and online. Smaller landlords simply cannot compete with that.

They can also acquire properties from smaller, unsophisticated landlords and then add a lot of value by simply changing the brand, management, and then integrating the property into their booking system, benefiting from their national advertising and revenue optimization models.

But now, the American market has become very competitive, which we expect to reduce future returns.

However, the European self-storage market is still in its early days, with just 1 square foot of storage space per capita, compared to 10 in the U.S., even as the concept is rapidly growing in popularity, offering REITs lots of lucrative opportunities to develop new properties.

Big Yellow is now replicating this successful model in Europe, and it has been very successful since its inception, earning 15%+ average annual total returns for its shareholders:

It currently has 11 new projects underway, which have the potential to expand its cash flow by 15-20% over the coming two years.

In addition to that, its current portfolio has a 20% vacancy because the storage market experienced some oversupply in the post-pandemic normalization period, meaning that it has significant capacity to lease space and grow revenue in its existing portfolio.

All in all, this has the potential to result in strong growth through 2030. Even then, the company is today trading at a near-decade low valuation of 16x FFO, down from 35x at its peak:

This multiple is particularly low when you consider that its LTV is just 10%.

We also think that self-storage is another property sector that should greatly benefit from the AI revolution, as it is expected to lead to significant job market disruption, causing people to lose their jobs, which has historically been a strong demand driver, as people need to move to find new jobs, creating demand for self-storage space.

As its growth accelerates and interest rates come back down, it could surge 30% just to get back to its 5-year mean valuation, and even that may not be enough given that it has the least leverage in its history.

Ignoring the upside potential, investors can expect double-digit total returns between the 5% dividend yield and 6-8% annual growth.

Closing Note

If I were going to invest in just a few REITs, I would stick to such blue-chip REITs with low leverage, Class A assets, and proven management teams.

There are lots of small-caps in the REIT sector that are even more heavily discounted, but they are also riskier, and you probably should stay away from them unless you are willing to build a well-diversified portfolio of 15-20 names and watch it closely.

That's what we do at High Yield Landlord, and our approach has resulted in significant outperformance over the long run, but this is not for everyone.

data.title: Alibaba's AI Revolution Meets Consumer Revival
data.description:
Thesis

Alibaba (NYSE:BABA) (OTCPK:BABAF) is slowly transforming into a two-engine company focused on consumption and AI. The core China commerce platforms are regaining steam with a faster, easier, richer Taobao, while the cloud division is translating rising AI demand into a real revenue scale. International retail and logistics are stitching it all together and are broadening Alibaba’s horizon beyond China. The outcome is a more straightforward narrative than what the market is currently valuing. The company is concentrating on one consumer platform and one infrastructure platform, supported by growing engagement, a richer membership ecosystem, and a clear capital return policy. This transition from a sprawling conglomerate into a user-first, AI-powered company is why I am optimistic about Alibaba over a multi-year timeframe. Management has set forth the strategy in clear language and is executing against it.

What the Market Misses

The latest investor conversation around whether Alibaba can navigate through macro noise and competition has become stale. The primary evolving factor is that Alibaba has reorganized to fit execution to two broad pillars. On the consumer side, the company has integrated Taobao, Tmall, Ele.me, and Fliggy into a single China commerce group and put one executive in charge of all of its e commerce brands globally, which drives down internal contention and accelerates product speed. On the infra side, Alibaba Cloud is going all in on generative AI via open source models, full stack tooling, and a deeper investment plan. The common thread is a universal architecture that utilizes AI to further customer experience, merchant tools, and operations throughout the organization. This is a structural, not cyclical, change in how Alibaba competes.

China’s Commerce Is Re-Accelerating Through Pace And Purity

Taobao has raised the pace and purity of experience. Taobao Instant Commerce, rolled out back in April, delivered true on demand retail to the main app so customers can buy and receive goods in as little as an hour. They also made larger investments in customer service and membership perks that create repeat purchase cycles. The combination of those measures produced a greater take rate and customer management revenues, with double-digit gains in recent quarters.

A Second Flywheel In Commerce

Alibaba is pivoting Amap from a navigation utility to a lifestyle access point. With its new Street Stars feature, which uses an AI to rank restaurants, hotels, and spots to visit, the company has also fully supported the launch with coupons and subsidies to generate usage. And it’s working. During China’s large National Day holiday time last week, Amap had a record of more than 360 million daily active users, which signals Amap is now a viable traffic engine for local merchants and a natural cross-sell channel for Ele.me and instant commerce. This increase follows a year in which the Amap and Local Services segments began to close losses on a greater scale and better unit economics.

Cloud And AI Are A Second Growth Engine, Not Optional

Alibaba Cloud is seeing a real, quantifiable AI cycle. AI product revenue has grown at a triple-digit rate for seven consecutive quarters. In the June quarter, cloud revenue growth increased to 26 percent year on year, and management indicated that AI has emerged as a significant component. It is not merely inference APIs. The company is experiencing more appetite for compute, storage, and public cloud resources for supporting AI adoption by some internet, retail, manufacturing, and media customers.

Alibaba’s Qwen family has also become possibly the largest open source model ecosystem in the world. The Qwen3 series' advanced reasoning capabilities were entirely open source for developer adoption acceleration. The approach is pretty simple. Take capable models, easily accessible, to draw developers into Model Studio and PAI, and monetize compute, tools, and vertical apps. For the China market, where U.S. export controls limit the supply of high-end GPUs, that flywheel is still in play because most enterprise workloads would be happy with small chips or export-compliant chips with an efficient software stack.

Financials And Capital Returns

The metrics suggest a returning growth company investing in the next appendage. For the March quarter, revenues increased by 7%, and adjusted EBITA grew 36%, with cloud growing faster and Taobao customer management revenue growing 12%. For FY 2025 as a whole, revenue is up 6% and operating income is up 24% as the company leaned into technology and commerce investments as part of the User First strategy. In June, Alibaba noted that cloud revenue growth accelerated to 26% and that International Commerce was up 19% — while cash and liquid investments were approximately 586 billion yuan at quarter close.

Valuing And The Path To Re Rating

Valuation hinges on whether investors conclude that Alibaba is once again a growth compounder. The case for multiple expansion is based on three observations. First, China commerce is reaccelerating with new features like instant retail and improved member benefits improving engagement and monetization. Second, cloud is beginning a multiyear upgrade cycle as enterprises roll out generative AI across workflows, and Alibaba is already tapping into this demand at scale. Third, international commerce is nearly breakeven with positive growth, which means profit should begin to be incremental with improved unit economics. When all three engines are running at the same time, earnings power grows faster than the headline revenue, and the combination of buybacks enhances the per share effect. That is the backdrop for multiple expansions from here.

Main Risks

There is real execution risk; instant commerce is capital intensive and competes against well capitalized competitors. Subsidies could reach a prisoners’ dilemma state, if spending discipline waivers, and profitability may suffer if the company spends too aggressively. The cloud strategy rests on heavy capex and assured access to a supply of capable AI chips, in a disorganized export controls environment. Domestic chip initiatives and export compliant GPUs help, but supply friction could slow model training or increase costs. The macro risk is China is always there. Consumer sentiment, housing markets and regulation affect the pace of recovery, and the ferocity of competition. Finally, international expansion in Europe has geopolitical and compliance complexity. Each are retreatable risk but worth keeping an eye on.

Catalysts

A number of visible catalysts can move the re-rating forward. On the consumer side, the continued integration of Taobao Instant Commerce into the Double 11 and Lunar New Year cycles should result in a higher frequency of orders and customer management revenue. Amap’s rapid user growth could translate into more local advertisement and cross-selling to Ele.me, which would help shift the revenue mix toward higher margin. On the infrastructure side, another quarter of at least 20 percent cloud growth and sustained triple-digit AI product growth would validate that Alibaba’s AI strategy is scaling successfully. Management has also signaled a desire to expand AI and cloud capex above the announced three-year plan, which demonstrates confidence in the demand. Ongoing buybacks and the potential for another annual dividend add to a steady capital return cadence along with these operational milestones.

Conclusion

Alibaba has regrouped around two businesses that are compounding well together. The consumer platform is becoming faster and stickier with a membership program that now has size. The cloud platform is capitalizing on the AI wave with open models, developer-friendly tools, and a willingness to spend. For investors that can get over the old story and think about how the company is operating as of today, the risk-reward is compelling.

data.title: Hold The Horses On Texas Pacific Land
data.description:
Introduction

Texas Pacific Land (NYSE: NYSE:TPL) shocked many investors when it became one of the best performers on the stock market in 2024. Most have never heard of the company that is synonymous with the oil & gas industry in the great state of Texas. With that in mind, I want to explore the company, understand its business model and establish a potential value thesis as the company seems to have gone too far away from valuation fundamentals.

Operation Overview

TPL is relatively unique in the American Energy landscape as it leverages its landholdings in the prolific and all-mighty Permian Basin. It separates its revenue into two distinct segments: Land & Resource Management and Water Services & Operations.

Land & Resource Management is the way the company can monetize its rich inheritance by maximizing the value of the surface as well as mineral rights. Basically, TPL holds perpetual royalty interests in thousands of acres that are drilled by E&Ps, allowing TPL to benefit from Oil extraction without having to fork out the capital-intensive costs and the risks of operating wells. TPL only has to keep strong relationships with operators and encourage the development of its overall acreage. Outside of O&G royalties, the company also provides easements, commercial leases and material leases, as well as sometimes selling land. The segment has generated the majority of revenue at $480.4MM over the LTM, with O&G royalties contributing nearly $400MM.

Water Services & Operations is the other facet of TPL’s business, which recognizes the key role of water in the modern O&G industry. It provides a variety of water solutions to various operators in the basin, which is a fairly synergistic segment with L&RM. It provides solutions such as Produced Water Management, Water Sourcing and Infrastructure Development. The segment has been responsible for the company's growth while crude prices have dipped, and it was responsible for $262.5MM in overall sales for the LTM.

Current Dynamics

TPL is one of the largest landowners in the state of Texas, with around 847,000 acres, and was originally created as a business trust in 1888 to manage the property of the Texas and Pacific Railway Company. The story drastically shifted in 2016 when the company started a new strategy to maximize its asset value through active management of surface and royalty interests. This allows TPL to have great FCF generation as it did not have to buy the lands but inherited them from the original lands near the railroad.

Now the fate of the company is heavily tied to Permian production, which has seen a massive increase in production since the rise of unconventional shale, with crude production standing at around 1MMBBL/d back in 2010 to now closing in on 7MMBBL/d. This translates to a near parabolic rise in producing horizontal wells on TPL acreage to now closing in on 7,000, and Net Royalty Production at 26.8MBOE/d. Looking ahead, the Permian’s production is set to grow, albeit at a slower rate than recent years, due to both geological maturity with most Tier 1 locations already being developed, as well as the industry facing a lower price environment, which does not drive drillers to increase their drilling activity.

This is why TPL is also counting on “SLEM” to be able to capture other revenue streams outside of O&G, as it can lease for renewable forms of energy such as solar, wind and carbon capture. Though this is only 10% of the company’s consolidated revenues for FY 2024, and the segment is mainly used for O&G-derived businesses, so there is time to see if the company can develop its renewable business. Regarding the Water sales business, it has grown tremendously for a relatively “side business”, reaching 3,435MBBL/d in total produced water royalty volumes, up from 88 in 2017. That’s also thanks to the Delwara Basin having a high water-oil ratio, further increasing with the lifespan of a well. This all ties with the company’s strategy to capture all available revenue over a well lifecycle without having to commit high operating expenditures.

Valuation

By relative financial multiples, TPL is expensive with its current P/E GAAP of 46.54, over 4 times Viper Energy (VNOM) and Black Stone Minerals (BSM), as well as a pricy EV/EBITDA of 34.17, where it is important to note that the company has no debt. The company’s business model requires close to no capex and regularly highlights its FCF. At current prices, I was surprised to find a mere FCF yield of 2.2%, highlighting how the stock has risen past fundamentals. Though this premium has generally been the case for TPL due to its unique position of owning both mineral and surface rights, while having virtually no debt. However, based on my growth assumptions for the company with the O&G royalties business flattening in the near future, and with a relatively bullish stance, my DCF modelling never gave me an implied share price that could justify this current price with these conditions, with the highest being at around $530.

Regarding growth, TPL has been able to grow its revenue at 7.71% CAGR over the past 3 years and its net income by 6.74% CAGR, while the industry has been grappling with falling crude prices since the peak back in 2022. Looking ahead, the Permian is set to see slower growth as the company is inextricably linked to the region’s long-term output and valuation multiples are linked to the parabolic rise in volume, which should not be the case in the future. Water Services & Operations is the most immediate growth driver, which should continue in the mid-term as TPL is actively deploying capital to build infrastructure crucial for non-conventional shale players, increasing earnings in a flat /lower commodity price dynamic. SLEM should be the long-term growth story for the company, though we will have to wait and see if it can create a strong relationship with renewable energy providers. Thus, with the kind of outlook for both crude and the Permian, I expect O&G royalties to dip in the remainder of the year, further depreciating share prices while the water business continues its growth. In the medium term, I expect O&G royalties to stay flat while the company continues to scale its other business, thus we could see a lower rate of return of capital to shareholders.

Profitability is a bright star for TPL as the company has close to no operating costs, as it simply leases its land for the bulk of its revenue, though it has expanded its water services, which have reduced operating margins over time from 86.9% in 2018 to now 73.8%. The company has been able to drastically improve its FCF margin over time to an astonishing level, and one of the highest on the market at 65.2% thanks to its asset-light model. And the great part of the TPL‘s business model would be that, despite lower realized prices, hurting O&G royalties, the company was able to boost all other aspects of its business, acting as a buffer. With my vision of the future for TPL, I believe that we will continue to see operating margins dip as the company becomes more and more reliant on capital measures in its water business to pick up the pace of the O&G royalty side, making its bottom line more in line with the rest of its peers, thus depreciating the valuation metrics.

TPL also drastically increased its capital return to shareholders since its IPO, with around $70MM back in 2018 to now over $376M in 2024. The dividend is fairly low at 0.69% though it has increased by a massive 13.94% CAGR over the past 5 years. The company also buys back its shares at opportune times when the market does not recognize the full value of the company and it is heavily focusing on FCF for shareholders, thus further boosting its capital return policy.

Conclusion

I believe that TPL is a great company with a rather unique business model. It is a company to buy at opportune times but the time is not right now in my opinion. It’s highly touted FCF metric only gives shareholders a 2.2% FCF yield at current prices, while its capital return policies are simply too small at current prices as well. The company is well-footed in the energy center of America, but with the recent headwinds, I believe that the stock price should be lower, which should then offer an opportunity for investors. I rate Texas Pacific Land as a Hold.

data.title: The Mint IPO: Spectacular Gains Obscure A Precarious Future
data.description:
Since its initial public offering (IPO), Mint Incorporation Limited’s (NASDAQ:MIMI) share price has nearly doubled, rising by 95%, compared to around 15% for both the Shares Russell 3000 ETF (IWV) and the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY). This spectacular stock performance is in stark contrast with the firm’s declining fundamentals. Added to that, there are new risks from the firm’s pivot to robotics that make the company more unattractive. With sky high valuations, there is enormous downside to the firm’s share price.

Business Model

Although it was incorporated in the British Virgin Islands on October 18, 2023, Mint’s operations are conducted in the Hong Kong Special Administration Region (SAR). Mint itself is a holding company, whose two subsidiaries, CKL Holding Ltd. and Aspiration Group Ltd., are also incorporated in the British Virgin Islands. However, Mint and these subsidiaries do not engage in any business. The company’s operating subsidiary, Matter Interiors Ltd., is organised under the laws of and operates in the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region (SAR), and was formed on November 16, 2018.

As Mint explains in its 20-F, through Matter Interiors Ltd., the company engages in interior design and is a fit-out works, “providing integrated and industry-specific interior design and fit-out works for commercial properties.” This work is done in office spaces across various industries, and in retail stores, and aims to reflect their clients' corporate values and reflect their brands. The company’s clients include many globally known offices, retail stores, food and beverage outlet chains, and locations owned by an important charity. The company also serves luxury residential properties in the SAR. These projects fall into two categories:

“(i) design services, in which we develop and create tailor-made interior design proposals; and (ii) design and fit out services, in which we undertake the overall project management, coordination and quality control, and supervise fit out works carried out by our subcontractors, complemented by other services such as repair and maintenance works and procurement of furniture and fit out materials, etc.”

In fulfilling its mission, the company offers design services and design and fit-out services. Design services involve the company’s designers learning about the particular needs of their customers, and the industries they operate in, and coming up with various design ideas that reflect what they have learned. These are edited into two-dimensional design proposals created through Adobe or AutoCAD that are presented to the customers. After an iterative process, a final three-dimensional design proposal is created using computer-aided design (CAD) systems. Design and fit-out services involve the just-discussed design services as well as fit-out services. This category made up the bulk of the firm’s projects in fiscal years (FY) 2023 and 2024. Fit-out services involve project managers monitoring the progress of design projects, supervising subcontractors, making on-site visits, and conducting quality inspections. Design and fit out services span the process from design services to rectifying defects in the defects liability period six to twelve months after the certificate of completion has been handed over.

Business tends to come from private companies and individuals, with recurring customers and referrals from current or previous customers providing the majority of business. When a project is initiated, customers pay a deposit of between 20% and 40% to the company. The initial phase generally lasts a month or less.

Growth Plan Is a Major Pivot

As part of its strategic growth plan, on Sept. 15, the company announced the launch of another operating subsidiary, Axonex Intelligence Limited, organised under the laws of Hong Kong. Touted as an "expansion into the technology realm with the fast-growing smart facility management sector”, Axonex is a way for Mint to “harness robotics, IoT, and AI technologies to enhance the way properties are managed and experienced.” It is not clear if Axonex has begun operations, as the language of the announcement shifts from the present to the future tense:

“Axonex provides total solutions for smart facility management, integrating advanced technologies to improve efficiency, safety, and user experience across different property types. Its key capabilities include:

  • Robotic solutions to automate routine facility management tasks, improve efficiency, and reduce manpower needs.

  • Object identification and human posture recognition for enhanced security and operational monitoring.

  • Digital twin solutions enabling real-time visualization and management of facilities.

  • AI-powered analytics to optimize maintenance, space utilization, and resource allocation.

Axonex is set to serve a diverse range of clients, including shopping malls, government premises, residential complexes, and warehouses. By leveraging its design background and new technological focus, Axonex aims to provide a holistic approach to property and facility management.”

Management believes that collaborations with tech partners such as Aishang Mobility Limited, and Sharp Peak Consulting Limited, will allow it to deliver meaningful integration of hardware and software. Theoretically, this should improve efficiencies, reduce costs, and allow boosted growth. However, it is notable that, prior to this announcement, there was no mention of robots, artificial intelligence, or even innovation, in the 20-F. The rudeness of the move, and the seeming lack of expertise in the area, despite management’s talk of a pedigree of innovation, make this pivot suspect. Execution risk will be high, although the tech partnerships will mitigate that to some extent, and caution is warranted because in the “build or buy” debate in tech, there is a view that it is cheaper for firms to simply use third-party technology rather than try to build it themselves. Margins may decline rather than increase, as a result of investments to build out this new subsidiary. It is not surprising that the share price has fallen from $10.75 at the close of trading on 15 Sept. to $7.78 at the time of writing.

Voluntary Lock-Ups Are A Show Of Confidence

The share price decline in the wake of the Axonex announcement seems to have triggered a show of confidence on the part of management and key shareholders. The recent announcement of voluntary lock-up agreements between the company and certain shareholders, including Mint’s chairman, Cheong Shing Ku, and its chief executive officer, Hoi Hung Chan, is a deliberate signal to the market that management and key shareholders believe in the direction of the company. The agreements cover some 26.7% of Mint’s class A shares, and 100% of its class B shares, with the lock-up period beginning on Sept. 30, 2025, and ending on March 31, 2026. Chan explained that

“These voluntary lock-up agreements demonstrate our confidence in Mint’s vision and the execution of our growth strategy. As Mint is extending its expertise to harness robotics, IoT, and AI technologies to enhance the way properties are managed and experienced, we believe this commitment will further reinforce long-term shareholder confidence and support sustainable value creation.”

This will have the unintended consequence of increasing the volatility of the share price: with fewer shares trading, the impact of sales or purchases will be increased. That is the short-term impact. That said, this show of confidence certainly shows that key people believe in this project.

Unimpressive Recent Financial Performance Looms Large

A key reason for this strategic pivot may be that the company’s overall financial performance has been rather unimpressive. Revenue has compounded by 15.54% a year since FY2022. However, revenue fell from $4.38 million in FY2024 to $3.27 million in FY2025, a decline of 25%. That is an alarming decline. The company’s net income declined from $0.26 million in FY2022 to -$1.46 million in FY2025. The loss in FY2025 was driven by a combination of a collapse in revenue and a surge in general and administrative expenses, from $669,825 in FY2024 to $2.3 million in FY2025. This led to a fall in net income from $784,618 in FY2024 to -$1.46 million in FY2025. In the 20-F, you can see that staff and benefit expenses were the biggest driver of growth in general and administrative expenses, followed by office expenses. Overall, management explained that the increase in general and administrative expenses was due to, “the increase in IPO bonus, listing expense, and office expense resulting from our initial public offering.”

Although one-time IPO related expenses will go down, this will be balanced against increases related to the costs of being a publicly traded company.

Sky High Valuation

At the current price, Mint has a price/sales ratio of 51.03. This is in stark comparison with Seeking Alpha’s estimate of the sector median of 1.68, and Prof. Aswath Damodaran’s sector median estimate of 1.26. If we consider that the company is coming off the back of declining revenue, and faces execution risks from a novel pivot into robotics, there is a rational basis for the view that the company’s valuation will decline. If that is the case, it should fall toward sector medians. If that happens, the company faces steep downsides.

Source: Seeking Alpha, Damodaran, and author calculations

As the table shows, if the company’s price/sales falls in line with sector multiples, it faces a 97% decline in its share price, giving us a target price of $0.22.

Conclusion

Mint presents investors with a stark paradox of spectacular past performance while showing falling growth, recent losses. The firm’s deteriorating fundamentals seem to be the primary catalyst for the company's radical pivot into smart facility management through its new subsidiary, Axonex. The firm’s strategic shift is a key issue. It represents a high-execution-risk venture into a technologically complex field where Mint has no established expertise. The subsequent 28% share price decline following the Axonex announcement reflects the market's justified skepticism. Although the voluntary lock-up agreements from management are a positive signal of confidence, they do little to mitigate the core operational and financial risks of this pivot. The immense challenge of building a viable technology company from the ground up, coupled with the likelihood of rising costs and compressed margins, casts serious doubt on Axonex's ability to drive growth. Overall, Mint’s valuation is wholly unsustainable. A price/sales ratio of 51.03, compared to a sector median of approximately 1.5, implies a level of future growth that is not supported by its recent financial performance or its high-risk new strategy. The mathematics are imposing; a reversion toward sector medians will lead to a catastrophic decline in the share price of approximately 97%. Consequently, Mint is a “Strong Sell.”

data.title: Beazer Homes USA: The Picture Has Worsened (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
A few months ago, in July of 2025, I decided to reaffirm Beazer Homes USA (NYSE:BZH) as a ‘strong buy’ candidate. This decision came in spite of weakness that the business was already exhibiting caused by pain in the housing market. I made the case, however, that the business would do well in the long run. Since then, however, the stock has dropped 4.4% at a time when the S&P 500 is up 5.3%. That's not a huge disparity, but it's not immaterial either. Looking at the data today, I continue to believe in the long term picture for the business. Having said that, the weakening that we have seen is disconcerting by this point. And with it likely to continue moving forward, I think the downgrading the enterprise to a ‘buy’ makes the most sense at this time.

Tough times

This is not a particularly pleasant time for players in the homebuilding market. Years of inflationary pressures, combined with high interest rates aimed at offsetting those, have proven to be rather destructive for the industry. As the chart above illustrates, home prices continue to trend lower. And in the charts below, you can see weakness in terms of new US home sales, housing completions, housing starts, and housing permits. This is something that I foresaw coming quite a while ago. But I did not expect for the pain to the bottom line of a company like Beazer Homes USA to be all that severe.

The most recent data that we have regarding Beazer Homes USA covers through the third quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue for the business came in at $545.4 million. That is down from the $595.7 million that the firm reported one year earlier. Even though the company benefited from a higher average price per home closed of $517,300 compared to the $505,300 that it generated a year earlier, the number of homes closed contracted from 1,167 to 1,035.

The drop in revenue is not surprising. But honestly, the decline on the bottom line is. The firm went from a net gain of $27.2 million in the third quarter of 2024 to a net loss of $0.3 million. A big pain point here ended up being the extra $10.1 million of inventory impairments and abandonments that the company booked during the quarter. But there were other areas of weakness as well.

As an example, even as revenue declined, general and administrative expenses grew from $49.7 million to $53.1 million. This was not the only area of weakness. As an example, while operating cash flow did improve from negative $83.4 million to negative $62.3 million, if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a plunge from $36.1 million to $14.1 million. In addition to this, EBITDA for the business contracted from $53.5 million to $32.1 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024. Revenue is actually still up, driven by a rise in the number of homes closed from 2,954 to 3,021 and by a rise in the average price per home closed from $510,900 to $513,700. What this indicates to me is that the weakness that the company has exhibited has been relatively fresh. That alone could indicate that we are in the earlier stages of some downward pressure from a fundamental standpoint. And just like what we saw with the third quarter on its own, the drop in revenue for the business brought with it meaningful declines in profits and cash flows.

There are some other worrisome signs that investors should be aware of. For instance, the company has seen a rise in its cancellation rate from 18.6% in the third quarter of 2024 to 19.8% the same time this year. On top of this, the number of net new homes ordered has fallen quite a bit from 1,070 last year to 861 this year. This, combined with elevated home deliveries has pushed the firm's backlog down from 1,949 to 1,352. The only positive thing here that I see is the fact that the average price of a home in backlog grew from $536,900 to $549,200.

For the rest of this year, management has not really provided much in the way of detail. They did say, however, that investors should expect EBITDA in the final quarter to be around $50 million. That would give us a full year reading of $144 million compared to the $243.4 million reported the previous year. And if we assume that the final quarter of this year will look like the first nine months did on a year over year basis, we would get a decline for adjusted operating cash flow from $174.8 million to $73.3 million.

This downward pressure is disappointing, but not wholly unexpected. Even in light of these challenges, it's important to note that management remains focused on their objectives. The primary objective I'm referring to here is the firm's push to grow to have over 200 active communities by 2027. That's up from the 162 it ended last year with. And it would represent a nice increase over the 175 that management is targeting for the current fiscal year.

I think it's going to be difficult, but not necessarily impossible, for the company to achieve this. Long term, I am very bullish on the housing market. The population of the US continues to grow and the size of the housing shortage is estimated to be about 4.7 million units. But in the near term, we should expect weakness. In the image below, you can see certain data provided by management. As new housing inventory units have risen in the last few years, so too has the monthly mortgage payment as a percentage of consumer income. These two things might seem at odds. However, it can be traced back to high interest rates that are being used to combat inflationary pressures.

At some point, this picture will ease. Even though I expect additional pain because I think the US is heading toward a recession, the good side to this is that a recession will bring about a faster cut in interest rates. As interest rates decline, housing will become more affordable absent a surge in price caused by bottled up demand. That could certainly help. But in recent years, it has been the interest rate that this played a larger role in making homes less affordable for the vast majority of consumers.

Because of the deterioration that we have seen on the bottom line, the firm is not as attractive as it once was. In the chart above, you can see how the company is priced using three different valuation metrics. This includes estimates for 2025 and historical results for 2024. On a forward basis for 2025, the company is not quite as attractive as it was previously. Relative to other similar firms, it is worth noting that the picture is mixed. In the chart below, I stacked it up against five such businesses. On a price to operating cash flow basis, only one of the five companies was cheaper than it is. But when it comes to the EV to EBITDA approach, our candidate is the most expensive of the group.

Some of this disparity stems from the fact that the net leverage ratio for Beazer Homes USA is anything but low. Management estimated that it was about 5.7 on a trailing 12 month basis as of the end of the most recent quarter. But if we use the forward estimates, it would rise to 7.31. In the chart below, you can see that this would put the company well above any of the five firms that I compared it to. This certainly justifies caution, especially since net debt is already meaningfully above the market capitalization of the business as of this writing.

Takeaway

Right now, I believe that investors should be prepared for some lumpiness in the homebuilding market. The fundamental picture is worsening a bit faster than I expected it to. Long term, I fully expect this industry to be solid. But in light of the fundamental change that we have seen here, I believe that downgrading Beazer Homes USA from a ‘strong buy’ to a ‘buy’ would be prudent at this time.

data.title: EQT Corporation: Sharp Increase In EBITDA Lays Foundation For Share Price Growth
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Today's realities in the US gas market allow major producers to exploit potential opportunities to generate additional profits. These include EQT Corporation (NYSE:EQT), whose financial results for the first and second quarters showed a significant improvement in the efficiency of its business model. My calculations allow me to conclude that the acquisition of new assets by Olympus Energy is effective, with expected growth in profits and free cash flow of +21.14% and +24.5%, respectively. Improved economic efficiency of operations and strategic production management are creating conditions that could lead to growth in EQT's shares. Moreover, growing demand for American gas creates a strong balance, on the basis of which forecasts for production and capital cost reductions are being raised. The financial model results for calculating the fair value of EQT Corporation revealed a discount to the premium of +46.8%. For this reason, my recommendation is to "Buy".

Overview Of EQT Corporation And Its Sales Strategy

EQT Corporation is a traditional gas company in the Appalachian Basin of the United States. It is engaged in a full range of production processes and services, from gas exploration and production to storage, transportation, and sales to end users. Major customers include the B2B sector (utility companies, industrial companies, data centers, and gas distributors). The business model of EQT Corporation is simple and involves running a straightforward gas business, where the key tools for managing competitiveness are the cost of production and the selling price of gas.

As a key indicator of EQT's business performance, its sales are significant. The total sales of natural gas and liquid petroleum products, including cash-settled derivative financial instruments, for the second quarter of 2025 generated $1.599 billion for the company. Of this amount, the majority ($1.438 billion) $1.438 billion came from natural gas sales. As such, the main focus is on gas production, storage, and sales. Compared to the second quarter of 2024, natural gas sales increased by $413 million (+28.7%). The key driver of sales growth is the increase in gas prices. The NYMEX price ($/MMBtu) in the second quarter of 2025 was $3.43, compared to just $1.92 a year earlier. Similarly, the spot price of natural gas rose from $2.02 to $3.63. EQT Corporation's average adjusted natural gas sales price is $2.88. The results for natural gas sales are presented in more detail below.

As far as EQT Corporation's financial stability is concerned, it is very attractive, as the company is demonstrating earnings growth above analysts' expectations. The Q2 financial results showed non-GAAP earnings per share (EPS) growth to $0.45, which is $0.05 higher. Meanwhile, revenue growth of +167.7% year-on-year increased it to $2.55 billion (which is $800 million higher than analysts' expectations). The important indicator for me in assessing EQT's investment attractiveness is the change in net debt. In Q2 2025, it decreased from $9.1 billion to $7.8 billion. Its size directly affects the determination of the company's fair value. Thus, we can conclude that the sharp increase in profits signals EQT Corporation's strategic advantage. More details on the company's financial information for Q2 2025 are provided below.

The Acquisition Of Olympus Energy Assets Will Enable EQT To Regain Its Leading Position In The US Gas Market

The primary reason for the expected profit growth in the coming quarters is related to the first financial results from the April decision to acquire Olympus Energy assets for $1.8 billion. This transaction is promising for EQT Corporation's business as it allows it to expand its production and sales volumes without complicating its business model. The organizational structure of EQT's management is vertically integrated. It allows the company to add assets with a similar economic profile while slightly increasing administrative costs.

For shares of EQT, this is an explainable factor for potential EBITDA growth. The management's expectations boil down to obtaining an average annual adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow without the use of borrowed funds over three years in the amounts of $530 million and $270 million, respectively. The Olympus Energy assets were expected to start operating on July 1, 2025, which will allow the first results of the integration to be reflected in the quarterly report for the third quarter of 2025 (expected to be published on October 22).

What is the appeal of this deal for EQT's investment attractiveness? Considering that EBITDA amounted to $2.507 billion in 2024, the $530 million increase from the acquisition of Olympus Energy's assets will bring EBITDA to $3.037 billion. This represents growth of +21.14%. Free cash flow without the use of borrowed funds in 2024 amounted to $1.102 billion. Therefore, the additional $270 million will increase this figure to $1.372 million in 2025. The growth will be +24.5%. The Olympus Energy assets are estimated to have a useful life of 10 years. The quality of the gas basins located on Olympus Energy's 90,000 acres of gas production land is considered by experts to be high, comparable to EQT's best assets.

EQT Share Growth Targets

To determine the target value of EQT shares, I conducted financial modeling using DCF. This model allows us to identify the fair value of the company. The result of the modeling will be comparative results, which will allow us to assess the level of investment attractiveness of EQT Corporation. For the forecast part of the DCF model, I chose a 5-year period (2025-2029). The key valuation tool is the discount rate. EQT Corporation's WACC is currently 7.2%. The expected growth in EQT Corporation's profits during the forecast period will depend on market conditions in the gas production sector.

The first half of 2025 financial results suggests that EBITDA will show strong growth of 30%. This is mainly due to the completion of the acquisition of Olympus Energy assets. Management has announced that it expects to generate additional EBITDA of $530 million in the first three years. The following table shows the free cash flow schedule for EQT Corporation, excluding borrowed funds.

It is worth noting that factors such as profit growth dynamics, changes in free cash flow, and WACC may affect the revision of the valuation in the coming quarters. The last metric depends on the current monetary policy of the Federal Reserve, which is becoming more accommodative, leading to expectations of lower interest rates.

Further, the terminal value of the company is estimated. This is the value of cash flows in the post-forecast period. Expected constant cash flow growth in the post-forecast period should be considered lower than the discount rate (7.2%). It is proposed to set it at 2%, which is the forecast for long-term US GDP growth. The result of the terminal value calculation is $44.269 billion. As EQT has 624.1 million shares outstanding, the DCF per share will be $81.85. Considering the price of EQT Corporation shares at the time of writing ($55.76), their fair value is 46.8% higher.

The Risks Of The Investment Thesis Remain Under Control And Are Decreasing

Examining EQT Corporation's financial profile and business strategy leads me to a positive assessment of the investment attractiveness of its shares. Of course, the key condition will be the growth in natural gas prices. Despite positive expectations, EQT Corporation's business model faces a number of risks that could worsen the company's actual financial results. The primary trigger, as I mentioned, is the price of natural gas. As of early fall 2025, natural gas market inventories had reached more than 6% above normal levels. That creates a potential headwind, reducing the prospects for gas price growth over the next 3-12 months.

Further risks include difficulties in pricing natural gas, given the emergence of new supply contracts for power plants and data centers. EQT Corporation's main customers are gas distributors and utilities. They market and process natural gas into electricity, which is then consumed by various customers. Data centers are becoming increasingly common among these consumers. In its Q2 2025 quarterly report, EQT management identified potential risks associated with the need to meet sustained demand. As the company is betting on a strategy where increased production volumes are linked to new supply contracts, its response to emerging demand growth may be slow. As a result, EQT Corporation will lose potential new customers who will go to competing organizations.

Even so, the company is successfully signing new supply agreements with data centers, whose demand is growing with the use of artificial intelligence software. The primary consumer is the data center markets in the southeastern United States, which are accessible to EQT via the Mountain Valley Pipeline. A potential increase in sales to data centers under new contracts could reach 1.5 billion cubic feet per day (excluding the pipeline's capacity of 2 billion cubic feet per day). These moves led to Citi recently raising its target price for EQT shares, as main competitor PT Expand doesn't have as good a handle on managing the risks tied to new supply and data center deals.

Continued risks associated with price volatility should also be noted. But these are decreasing, as confirmed by historical data from 2022. “Average historical volatility of natural gas in the US tended to decline during the first half of 2025, with quarterly volatility falling from a recent high of 81% in the fourth quarter of 2024 to 69% in the second quarter of 2025.”

Conclusion

In summary, the results of my fundamental analysis of EQT Corporation shares indicate that they are highly attractive for investment. I recommend EQT as a “buy.” The projected growth value of their shares is $81.85 (+46.8%). The main factors include the integration of Olympus Energy's assets into EQT's business model, the conclusion of new supply agreements for data centers, the stabilization of natural gas prices, and a decrease in their market volatility.

data.title: Novo Nordisk: The Bottom Is In
data.description:
Since my last Novo Nordisk (NVO) analysis, the stock has lost 10.5% in price. We are in official deep value territory here, and I am considering buying, as I firmly believe the bottom is in.

I did buy NVO earlier in the year, but I sold it shortly afterward as I realized that I was too early and that downward pressure would persist for some time. Nonetheless, now that the price has had further time to deflate, I am certainly considering this as an opportune entry point as a deep-value investment to capitalize on the 2026 healthcare positive re-rating cycle. Novo Nordisk will never be Eli Lilly (LLY) (of which I own a 15%-weight stake in), but it can still be owned for good healthcare exposure amid the current favorable macro market dynamics for the industry from a pre-inflection investor's point of view.

To begin with, Novo Nordisk stock is resoundingly cheap right now. The 14-month RSI recently breached 30, which is about as oversold as a company can get, and is generally when elite deep-value sharks begin accumulating as long as the business isn't in genuine distress or facing long-term contraction and failure (NVO isn't).

Fundamentals & Valuation

NVO's forward non-GAAP P/E ratio is 14.5% lower than the sector median, but forward diluted EPS growth is 66% higher than the sector median. However, looking longer term, there's really a -30% under-sector difference in EPS growth on consensus right now. NVO is still positioned for alpha once funds flow into healthcare stocks in 2026 as rates are cut and real macro growth resumes, following the sentiment-based and Mag7-driven AI capex upcycle we've already been witnessing. However, NVO should be viewed as a sentiment-relative deep value investment. It's not fundamentally mispriced based on consensus; personally I do think the consensus is too pessimistic, but even then the valuation is not intrinsically deeply inefficient in any sense. Perhaps more accurately, NVO was grossly overvalued prior to this drawdown. To be clear, I don't expect the AI upcycle to end, just to moderate, as substantial liquidity shifts to value sectors as confidence returns with cooling inflation and a lower cost of capital.

I've recently written on Lilly's Orforglipron as a major sentiment catalyst in 2026, and I have mentioned that it will very likely dwarf Novo's impact considering that Novo is offering a product that requires being taken on an empty stomach with no more than 4 oz of water, while Lilly's can basically be taken without restriction. Novo's product is called Rybelsus (oral semaglutide). The data currently shows that high-dose oral semaglutide meaningfully improves efficacy while also removing the uncomfortable needle. The drug is already on the market and I expect it to be an ongoing, albeit moderate, support to the company's investor sentiment.

Rybelsus is now a $3.5 billion diabetes brand. It grew rapidly in 2022–23 but plateaued in 2024–25 as U.S. sales softened. Ex-U.S. expansion and positive cardiovascular outcomes should help offset declines, and the high-dose oral semaglutide now filed for obesity (which may also be branded under Rybelsus) will set the stage for the next leg of returns.

Looking at Novo's business more broadly, diabetes and obesity care are about 90% of revenue, with GLP-1 drugs (Ozempic, Wegovy, and Rybelsus) as the core engine. Rare diseases like haemophilia and growth hormone deficiency make up about 10%, which provides diversification but doesn't drive growth. But in essence, Novo is essentially a semaglutide/GLP-1 franchise.

Wegovy is the fastest-growing drug, and its up 56% in 2024. Cagrisema is in late-stage trials with ~23% weight loss, and so potentially the next blockbuster. As mentioned earlier, high-dose oral semaglutide is under FDA review for obesity, which extends the platform to a broader market.

Price Return Prospects

We should be conservative here. This is a company facing brutal downward revisions as the dominant Eli Lilly establishes itself as the GLP-1 GOAT. That's not fun for Novo as a company that has long been known as the go-to diabetes and metabolic healthcare specialist. That said, for opportunistic investors, we know how to spot a value opportunity. And no, this is not a value trap. The growth horizon is too clear thematically, macroeconomically, and based on the company specifics.

I will forecast a very moderate $4.20 for Novo's normalized EPS in Fiscal 2027, ending December 2027. This is below the consensus and reflects the reality of Lilly's dominance even amid a strong macro growth opportunity. I'm also considering a forward non-GAAP P/E re-rating toward the sector median of 18. I will use 17 for conservatism. This leads to a stock price target of $71.50 in about 12 months in a very, very conservative base case. The current stock price is $58.85, so the implied upside is 21.5% from here. I think in a bull case upside could easily be 30% if the multiple re-rates along with a general sector re-rating. The downside from here is asymmetrically unlikely, given macroeconomic factors like rate cuts on the horizon and the undervalued healthcare industry due for its 2026 re-rating.

Risks

Let's put the current climate for Novo into context. The company's 2025 sales outlook was reduced because U.S. penetration of branded GLP-1s lagged expectations, and the company specifically blamed growth of compounded, non-branded GLP-1s. Yet, there are ongoing risks even as the GLP-1 dynamics have cleared due to supply capacity constraints removing patent loopholes for compounders. Medicare price negotiations under the U.S. Inflation Reduction Act and similar cost-control measures abroad could compress margins on Wegovy and future oral GLP-1s on an ongoing basis.

Also, I think concentration on semaglutide is a no-brainer risk to consider. That is why when I bought Novo shares again this week, I sized it at a 4% weight, compared to my 15% weight for Eli Lilly. One is simply a more structurally resilient business, with much better diversification and in my opinion, far better leadership. Heavy reliance on a single molecule increases vulnerability, there is no question about it. An adverse safety signal, patent challenge, or superior competitor could disproportionately undermine earnings.

Conclusion: Strong Buy

Novo is now firmly in deep value territory, and I am telling you from all of my experience and developed intuition, the bottom is in, and the inflection has begun. This is an asymmetrical opportunity ahead of the 2026 healthcare re-rating. The stock is trading at a rare valuation dislocation. I'm forecasting a 21.5% in an ultra conservative base case. And I'm betting on a 30% upside in a bull-case outcome where macro tailwinds compound positively and industry re-rating sparks a Novo re-rating to a froward non-GAAP P/E of 18–19. Novo Nordisk might not be Eli Lilly, but it is a timely, deep-value allocation that elite investors will want to be privy to as we head into the coming healthcare rotation.

data.title: 5 Relatively Secure And Cheap Dividend Stocks, Yields Up To 8% (Oct. 2025)
data.description:
************

Author's Note: This is our monthly series on Dividend Stocks, usually published in the first week of every month. We scan the universe of roughly 7,500 stocks listed and traded on U.S. exchanges and use our proprietary filtering criteria to select five relatively safe stocks that may be trading cheaper compared to their historical valuations. Some of the sections in the article, like "Selection Process/Methodology," are repeated each month with few changes and are presented in "italics." This is intentional as well as unavoidable, as this is necessary for the new readers to be able to conceptualize the process. Regular readers of this series could skip such sections to avoid repetitiveness and jump to the section titled "Selection of the Top 50". Readers can also read a more detailed explanation of the goals and the selection process in our blog post here.

************

Introduction and Market Summary:

The market has recently made new all-time highs on the heels of the Fed's first rate cut in almost a year and expectations of two more rate cuts in 2025. The rate cut of 25 basis points was widely expected and, to some extent, was already priced in the market. More important was the Fed's indications of further rate cuts. Also, in recent months, the tariff concerns have taken a back seat. It is not that all has been settled; in fact, far from it. The litigation in US courts over the tariffs is ongoing and is now pending with the Supreme Court. But, at the same time, the U.S. administration has made some high-profile trade deals, especially with some major trading partners, such as Japan, South Korea, and the European Union, even though the details may be sketchy. But the market has shifted its focus mostly to the Fed policy and some new worries. More recently, there has been a new worry about the threat of a possible government shutdown. So far, it has not been taken very seriously, as Congress has a history of making deals and compromises at the 12th hour, and the hope is that they will avoid the shutdown. Nonetheless, the market is taking a pause to a certain extent after an extended rally. If the government shutdown is avoided and the Fed delivers another set of rate cuts this year, we may see the S&P500 cross the 7000 psychological level by the end of the year, barring any unforeseen shocks.

As the housing market, as well as the health of the broader economy, will depend greatly on what the Fed will do or not do in the coming months, you can see the current odds of the Fed's next rate cut here on the CME FedWatchTool website.

It is always good to have some idea of where the markets and the broader economy may be headed. Even so, we believe markets are always unpredictable, and it is not possible to catch the exact bottom (or the peak). So, it is best to invest regularly and consistently in good, solid dividend-paying stocks when their valuations are reasonable or attractive.

This month, we highlight three groups with five stocks each that have an average dividend yield (as a group) of 3.56%, 6.49%, and 8.19%, respectively. The first list is for conservative and risk-averse investors, while the second is for investors seeking higher yields but still wanting relatively safe dividends. The third group is for yield-hungry investors but comes with an elevated risk, and we urge investors to exercise caution.

The Selection Process:

Goals and Objectives:

The main goal of this series of articles is to shortlist and highlight companies that have a solid history of paying and raising dividends. In addition, we demand that these companies support strong fundamentals, carry low debt, and are offered at a relatively cheaper valuation. These DGI stocks are not going to make anyone rich overnight, but if your goal is to attain financial freedom by owning stocks that would grow dividends over time, meaningfully and sustainably, then you are at the right place. These lists are not necessarily recommendations to buy, but a shortlist of probable candidates for further research. Besides, we think each month, if nothing else, this analysis is able to highlight some nuggets that would have otherwise skipped our attention.

Every month, we start with roughly 7,500 stocks that are listed and traded on U.S. exchanges, including over-the-counter (OTC) networks. Using our filtering criteria, the initial list is quickly narrowed to roughly 700 stocks, mostly dividend-paying and dividend-growing. From there on, by using various data elements, including dividend history, payout ratios, revenue growth, debt ratios, EPS growth, etc., we calculate a "Dividend Quality Score" for each stock that measures the relative safety and sustainability of the dividend. In addition to dividend safety, we also seek cheaper valuations. We also demand that the selected companies have an established business model, solid dividend history, manageable debt, and investment-grade credit rating.

Notes: 1) Please note that when we use the term "safe" in relation to stocks and investments, it should be interpreted as "relatively safe" because nothing is absolutely safe in investing. Even though we present only 5 to 10 stocks in our final list, one should have 15-20 stocks at a minimum in a well-diversified portfolio.

2) All tables in this article are created by the author unless explicitly specified. The stock data have been sourced from various sources such as Seeking Alpha, Yahoo Finance, GuruFocus, Morningstar, and CCC-List (Dividend Radar).

Criteria to Shortlist:

  • Market cap > $7 billion (some exceptions can be made)
  • Dividend yield > 1.0% (some exceptions are made to include high-quality but lower-yielding companies)
  • Daily average volume > 100,000
  • Dividend growth past five years >= 0.

By applying the above criteria, we got around 600 companies.

Narrowing Down the List

As a first step, we would like to eliminate stocks that have less than five years of dividend growth history. We cross-check our current list of over 600 stocks against the list of so-called Dividend Champions, Contenders, and Challengers originally defined and created by David Fish. Generally, the stocks with more than 25 years of dividend increases are called dividend Champions, while stocks with more than ten but less than 25 years of dividend increases are termed Contenders. Further, stocks with more than five but less than ten years of dividend increases are called Challengers. Also, since we want a lot of flexibility and wider choice at this initial stage, we include some companies that pay dividends lower than the S&P500 but otherwise have a stellar dividend record and growing dividends at a fast pace.

After we apply all the above criteria, we're left with roughly 400 companies on our list. However, so far in this list, we have demanded five or more years of consistent dividend growth. But what if a company had a very stable record of dividend payments but did not increase the dividends from one year to another? At times, some of these companies are foreign-based companies, and due to currency fluctuations, their dividends may appear to have been cut in U.S. dollars, but in reality, that may not be true at all when looked at in the actual currency of reporting. At times, we may provide some exceptions when a company may have cut the dividend in the past, but otherwise, it looks compelling. So, by relaxing some of the conditions, roughly 60-80 additional companies were considered to be on our list. We call them category 'B' companies. After including them, we end up with roughly 450 plus companies that make up our first list.

We then import the various data elements from many sources and assign weights based on different criteria as listed below:

  • Current yield: Indicates the yield based on the current price.
  • Dividend growth history (number of years of dividend growth): This provides information on how many years a company has paid and increased dividends on a consistent basis. For stocks under the category 'B' (defined above), we consider the total number of consecutive years of dividends paid rather than the number of years of dividend growth.
  • Payout ratio: This indicates how comfortably the company can pay the dividend from its earnings. We prefer this ratio to be as low as possible, which would indicate the company's ability to grow the dividend in the future. This ratio is calculated by dividing the dividend amount per share by the EPS (earnings per share). The cash-flow payout ratio is calculated by dividing the dividend amount paid per share by the cash flow generated per share.
  • Past five-year and 10-year dividend growth: Even though it's the dividend growth rate from the past, this does indicate how fast the company has been able to grow its earnings and dividends in the recent past. The recent past is the best indicator that we have to know what to expect in the next few years.
  • EPS growth (average of previous five years of growth and expected next five years' growth): As the earnings of a company grow, more than likely, dividends will grow accordingly. We will take into account the actual EPS growth of the previous five years and the estimated EPS growth for the next five years. We will add the two numbers and assign weights.
  • Chowder number: So, what's the Chowder number? This number has been named after the well-known SA author Chowder, who first coined and popularized this factor. This number is derived by adding the current yield and the past five years' dividend growth rate. A Chowder number of "12" or more ("8" for utilities) is considered good.
  • Debt/equity ratio: This ratio will tell us about the company's debt load in relation to its equity. We all know that too much debt can lead to major problems, even for well-known companies. The lower this ratio, the better it is. Sometimes, we find this ratio to be negative or unavailable, even for well-known companies. This can happen for a myriad of reasons and is not always a reason for concern. This is why we use this ratio in combination with the debt/asset ratio (covered next).
  • Debt/asset ratio: This ratio will tell us about the debt load in relation to the company's total assets. In almost all cases, this ratio would be lower than the debt/equity ratio. Also, this ratio is important because, for some companies, the debt/equity ratio is not a reliable indicator.
  • S&P's credit rating: This is the credit rating assigned by the rating agency S&P Global and is indicative of the company's ability to service its debt. This rating can be obtained from the S&P website.
  • PEG ratio: This is also called the price/earnings-to-growth ratio. The PEG ratio is considered to be an indicator if the stock is overvalued, undervalued, or fairly priced. A lower PEG may indicate that a stock is undervalued. However, PEG for a company may differ significantly from one reported source to another, depending on which growth estimate is used in the calculation. Some use past growth, while others may use future expected growth.
  • Distance from 52-week high: We want to select companies that are good, solid companies, but also are trading at cheaper valuations currently. They may be cheaper due to some temporary down cycle, or some combination of bad news, or simply having a bad quarter. This criterion will help bring such companies (with a cheaper valuation) near the top as long as they excel in other criteria as well. This factor is calculated as (current price - 52-week high) / 52-week high.
  • Sales or Revenue growth: This is the average growth rate in annual sales or revenue of the company over the last five years. A company can only grow its earnings power as long as it can grow its revenue. Sure, it can grow the earnings by cutting costs, but that can't go on forever.

Selection Of The Top 50

Downloadable Dataset: Below, we provide a link to the table with relevant data on nearly 450 stocks. Readers can download this table for further analysis. Please note that the table is sorted by "Total Weight" or "Quality Score."

File-for-Export_-_5_DGI_-_Oct_2025.xlsx

We will first bring down the list (of 400-plus names) to roughly 50 names by automated criteria, as listed below. In the second step, which is mostly manual and subjective, we will bring the list down to about 15.

  • Step 1: First, take the top 20 names from the above table (based on total weight or quality score). At times, some industry segments tend to get overcrowded at the top, so we take the top two (or three) and ignore the rest.
  • Step 2: As a second step, we will take the top 10 names based on the highest dividend yield. When it comes to dividend yield, some of the industry segments tend to be overcrowded. So, we will take the top two (or a maximum of three) names from any single industry segment.
  • Step 3: Now, we will sort our list based on five-year dividend growth (highest at the top) and select the top 10 names.
  • Step 4: Not all companies have a 10-year streak of dividend growth, but many do. We will sort our list based on 10-year dividend growth (highest at the top) and select the top 10 names.
  • Step 5: We also want to give priority to stocks that are rated highest in terms of credit rating. So, we will sort the list based on the numerical weight of the credit rating and select the top 10 stocks with the best credit rating. Again, we are careful not to have too many names from the same sector.
  • Step 6: Lastly, as the name of the series suggests, we want to have some names that may be trading cheaper in comparison to their historical valuation. So, we select the top ten names with the highest discount. However, they could be trading cheap for just the wrong reasons, so we need to be careful that they meet our other quality criteria.

From the above steps, we now have a total of 70 names in our final consideration. However, the following stocks appeared more than once:

Stocks that appeared two times: ADP, CAG, CTRA, LYB, MSFT, PFE, STZ, UNH, and UPS (9 duplicates).

After removing eight duplicates, we are left with 61 (70-9) names.

Since we still have a very large list, we will remove names that have a low yield and not very great dividend growth. So, if the dividend yield was less than < 2.5% and the dividend growth (average of 5-year and 10-year) was less than 8%-10%, we would rather remove them at this stage.

So, we remove WMT, GWW, RL, AMP, BK, and ATO. After removing them, we are left with 55 names.

Since there are still multiple names in each industry segment, we will keep a maximum of two or three names (from the top) from any one segment.

On this basis, we remove a total of 16 names: EMN, MOS, BAH, MSCI, AMAT, OTIS, BF.A, ZION, BRO, ZURVY, FSV, MLI, LLY, CI, FANG, and COST.

After we remove these 16 names, we are left with 39 (55-16) names.
