Final Step: Narrowing Down To Just Five Companies

In this step, we construct three separate lists of five stocks each, with different sets of goals, dividend income, and risk levels.

The lists are:

1) Relatively Safe (Low-yield) Dividend list,

2) Moderately High Dividend List,

3) Ultra High Dividend List, and

4) A combined list of the above three (duplicates removed).

Out of the top 40 (41 this time), we make our judgment calls to make these three lists, so basically, the selections are based on our research and perceptions. So, while most of the filtering until now was based on automated criteria, the last step is pretty much subjective. We try to make each of the three lists highly diversified among various sectors and industry segments, and try to ensure that the safety of dividends matches the overall risk profile of the group. We certainly encourage readers to do further research on the highlighted names.

Nonetheless, here are our three final lists for this month:

Final A-List (Relatively Safe Income):

Criteria for this group:

  • The average yield for the group is between 2.75% and 4.25%.
  • At least three or four names with an 'A' rating (from S&P)
  • Solid track record of dividends and growth.

Average yield: 3.56%

  • (INTU)
  • (UNH)
  • (MS)
  • (CVX)
  • (UPS)

Table-1A: A-LIST (Conservative Income)

We think this set of five companies (in the A-List) would form a solid diversified group of dividend companies that would be appealing to income-seeking and conservative investors, including retirees and near-retirees. The average yield is 3.56%, which is more than 3x of what the S&P 500 pays. The average dividend history is 20 years. Also, all five companies have an excellent rating of 'A-' or higher. If you must need higher dividends, consider the B-List or the C-List, presented later.

As usual, we will provide a brief synopsis of each of the companies selected here; however, we will not go into too many details, as that will make the article unduly long.

INTU (Intuit Inc):

The current dividend yield of Intuit is very low at 0.60%, so it does not serve the purpose of retirees who may depend on dividends for their income. But we include it in the A-list as one of the five stocks, and primarily for dividend growth. The dividend growth has been close to 20% over the last five and 10-year periods. TurboTax and QuickBooks are its two major premium products leading the charge.

Intuit appears to be firing on all cylinders and has achieved 20% revenue growth in the fourth quarter and 16% growth on a full-year basis, both beating management's own guidance. The operating margins have also been expanding nicely. There are some risks and competitive threats, such as the IRS's free "Direct File" program. However, it poses minimal risk to Intuit's business, as the software is very basic and can address only simple returns.

UNH (UnitedHealth Group):

UnitedHealth is an integrated healthcare company that has operated in multiple business segments, such as healthcare insurance, pharmacy benefit management, health provider, and health analytics. This approach provided an outstanding record of performance and shareholder returns until this year. The company fell from grace with many allegations surrounding its healthcare denial practices and controversies around its Medicare Advantage programs. This has led to an antitrust investigation along with an intense regulatory scrutiny targeted at the company's practices.

Due to the ongoing issues, the market price has fallen sharply this year by nearly 55%, but has recently made some recovery. Morningstar recently lowered its fair value estimate to roughly $400 a share; the current price is still 11% lower.

Sure, the company is facing many headwinds, and margins in some of the segments may be lower permanently or may take time to recover, but patient investors can still benefit from a possible turnaround in a year or two. In the meantime, a 2.5% plus dividend yield can help. So, only patient investors should consider it after due diligence.

CVX (CHEVRON):

CVX needs no introduction as an oil major and is probably one of the best investments in its sector for stable income. However, earnings and the share price of the company are obviously tied to the movement of the oil prices, which can fluctuate widely based on geopolitical situations of the day. That being said, the company provides relative safety, an excellent credit rating, stable business, and a reasonably high dividend yield for a large company. Chevron is known to be a low-cost producer with its breakeven price in the low to mid-40s.

Similar to previous months, we are including CVX in the A-list for reasons stated above. CVX currently offers a highly attractive dividend yield of close to 4.3%. The payout ratio is in the low 60s on an EPS basis and only in the 40s on a cash flow basis. The dividend growth has been a bit tepid over the years, but reasonable at 6% plus, considering the starting yield. The company maintains an excellent credit rating of 'AA' from S&P, which shows its financial strength. Investors who do not mind lower yield from an Oil company but higher dividend growth should look at Imperial Oil (IMO).

MS (Morgan Stanley):

Morgan Stanley has been scoring well in our ratings since last year. However, it is no longer cheap (or undervalued) as the price has moved up significantly during the past year. In fact, Morningstar rates the share prices as roughly 20% overvalued. For this reason, it should only be accumulated on a dollar-cost-average basis.

That being said, we see many positives with the company. It is one of the largest investment banks when you count its revenue. It has roughly $5 trillion in clients' assets, with a majority of it under the supervision of their financial advisors. The past 5-year and 10-year dividend growth has been exceptionally good at over 21% and 23%, respectively, and the payout ratio is still very reasonable at 42%. However, we are unlikely to see the same level of dividend growth in the future, as the dividend growth has moderated in the last couple of years.

UPS (United Parcel Service):

UPS is one of two duopolies in the logistics sector in the US (the other one is FedEx). In fact, there are only three major multinational firms in the world: UPS, FedEx, and DHL (based in Europe). However, the company's share price has lost nearly 40% of its value during the last 52 weeks. Due to the falling share price, the dividend yield has gone up to almost 8%. At the same time, the payout ratio is uncomfortably high at over 80%. The negative sentiment is a combination of many factors. First, it is in the process of ramping down the Amazon package business, as the margins were very thin. Secondly, the tariff-related concerns, especially with the expiry of the tariff exemption on de-minimis packages, have added to the woes. Thirdly, the agreement negotiated by UPS with its employee unions in 2023 is proving to be expensive and driving wage cost inflation. The management is not sitting idle, though, and the company has undertaken downsizing and cost-cutting initiatives; however, it is yet to be seen how successful it will be. On the positive side, UPS maintains a solid balance sheet and maintains an excellent credit rating of 'A' from the S&P. Its debt is relatively high but manageable. As per Morningstar, the share price is nearly 25% below its fair value, even after Morningstar lowered its fair-value estimates. The current yield of 7.8% is nearly two and a half times its long-term 5-year average of 3.1%. Even though the company has termed the dividend as "rock-solid" in the last earnings call, it is not reassuring when the payout ratio is reaching nearly 100% of earnings.

In a nutshell, the company is more of a turnaround story right now, and this is the high-risk, high-reward stock we have included in the A-list. However, we believe patient investors are likely to be rewarded as the shares appear to be at or near the bottom, but the execution risk remains.

Final B-List (High Yield, Moderately Safe):

Criteria for this group:

  • Yield for the group between 4.75% to 6.50%
  • At least two names with 'A-' (or above) rated.
  • Above-average track record of dividends and growth

Average yield: 6.49%

  • (UNH)
  • (TGT)
  • (MPLX)
  • (UPS)
  • (ARCC)

Note 1: Very often, we include a few low-risk stocks in the B-list and C-list. Also, oftentimes, a stock can appear in multiple lists. This is done on purpose. We try to make each of our lists fairly diversified among different sectors/industry segments of the economy. We try to include a few of the highly conservative names in the high-yield list to make the overall group much safer.

Table-1B: B-LIST (High Yield)

NOTE: MPLX is structured as a partnership (not a corporation) and issues a K-1 tax form instead of the usual 1099-Div. Please use due diligence.

In the B-List, the overall risk profile of the group becomes slightly elevated compared to the A-List. That said, the group (as a whole) will likely provide safe dividends for many years. This list offers an average yield of 6.5% and an average of 25 years of dividend history and 20 years (average) of consecutive growth.

Some of our high-yield selections often repeat in the B-list and C-list.

MPLX (MPLX LP):

For the B-List and C-List, the dividend yield of Chevron is somewhat low. We replace it with MPLX LP, a highly attractive name from the midstream energy industry, mainly to elevate the yield. Please note that the company has a Partnership structure and issues a K-1 tax form (partnership income) instead of the usual 1099 in case of regular dividends.

We have covered MPLX multiple times in our previous articles in this monthly series. Hence, we will skip the details here. MPLX regularly features on our B-list and C-list due to its stable dividend growth and high yield. There are other good alternatives, such as Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) or Energy Transfer (ET).

ARCC (Ares Capital):

We are including Ares Capital in the B-list and the C-list. ARCC is one of the largest BDCs (Business Development Companies) in terms of market capitalization and net asset value. The company invests in small and medium-sized companies and has a diversified portfolio of investments in over 500 companies. Besides, ARCC has a proven record of performing reasonably well both in high and low-interest rate environments. Pricing and valuation-wise, it trades at a slight premium for being one of the largest in the BDC arena, and its stable dividend.

It generates a solid amount of NII (net Investment income), which currently exceeds well above the $1.2 billion mark on an annual basis. ARCC currently offers a highly attractive yield of roughly 9.4%.

Final C-LIST (Yield-Hungry, Less Safe):

Criteria for this group:

  • The average yield for the group is between 7.0% to 8.0%
  • All names with investment-grade ratings.
  • Some potential for growth.

Average yield: 8.19%

  • (TGT)
  • (MPLX)
  • (UPS)
  • (LYB)
  • (ARCC)

Notes:

Note 1: Oftentimes, a stock can appear in multiple lists. We try to include one or two conservative names in the high-yield list to make the overall group much safer.

Table-1C: C-LIST (Yield-Hungry, Elevated Risk)

NOTE: MPLX is structured as a partnership (not a corporation) and issues a K-1 tax form instead of the usual 1099-Div. Please use due diligence.

=====

It is apparent from the selections that this list (C-List) is for yield-hungry DGI investors. The yield goes up to 8.2%. However, please note that this list is not for conservative investors. As you can see, the average credit rating of this set of companies is much lower than the A-List or even the B-List. Dividends are moderately safe in this list, but not nearly as safe as in the A-List or B-list. At least a couple of companies in this list come with an elevated level of risk, as they are turnaround stories. We urge due diligence to determine if it would suit your personal situation. Nothing comes for free, so there will be more risk involved with this group. Even then, it's a diversified group spread among five different industry segments.

LYB (LyondellBasell Industries, N.V.):

LyondellBasell is one of the prominent companies in the materials sector and appears to be undervalued. It has been trading at attractive levels for some time, but the prices have undergone further correction due to the expectation of a global economic slowdown. However, the company is dealing with economic pressures by cutting costs, reducing capital spending, and moving further production to North America. The current yield is very tempting at nearly 11%. Please note that there is a moderate to high risk that the company comes under further pressure and cuts its dividends, which it has avoided steadfastly so far (unlike its peers). However, even if the dividend is cut, it is likely to remain north of 7% to 8%, but the price may come under further pressure. Considering these risks, only patient and risk-tolerant investors should consider it.

We are including LYB in our C-list because of the high yield and undervaluation. Morningstar currently assigns a five-star valuation rating to LYB. Morningstar's own fair-value estimates for LYB have come down in the last year; even then, the current market price is roughly 45% below their estimates.

LYB deals in petrochemicals and commodity chemicals and has a large manufacturing presence in North America, which provides it access to relatively cheaper natural-gas-based feedstocks even though it is headquartered in the Netherlands. The petrochemicals sector has been facing cyclical headwinds in general, as well as pricing pressures from new production capacity in Asia.

Note: We would like to caution that each company comes with certain risks and concerns. Sometimes, these risks are real, but other times, they may be a bit overblown and temporary. So, it's always recommended to do further research and due diligence.

What If We Were to Combine the Three Lists?

If we combine the three lists and remove the duplicates (because of combining), we would be left with nine names.

Two-time duplicates: ARCC, MPLX, TGT, and UNH (4 duplicates).

Three-time duplicates: UPS (2 duplicates)

After removing these SIX duplicates, we are left with nine names.

The combined list is highly diversified in as many as eight different industry segments. However, there may be multiple names for some of the sectors. The stats for the group of 9 are as follows:

Average yield: 5.65%

Average discount (from 52WK High): -24.2%

Average 5-yr dividend growth: 10.9%

Average 10-yr dividend growth: 12.2% (from 8 names)

Average Payout Ratio: 52%

Average Total Quality Score: 69.8

Table 2:

Conclusion:

In this article, we have presented three groups of dividend-paying stocks (five each) with different goals in mind to suit the varying needs of a wider audience. Even though the risk profile of each group is different, each group in itself is fairly balanced and diversified.

This month, the first group yields 3.56%, while the second group elevates the yield to 6.49%. We also presented a C-list for yield-hungry investors with an 8.19% yield. The combined group (all three lists combined with duplication removed) offers an even more diversified group with nine positions and a 5.65% yield.

data.title: Enbridge: The Indirect AI Winner
data.description:
Investment thesis

My bullish call on Enbridge (NYSE:ENB) back in March has worked out well. The stock has returned around 23% since then, beating the broader U.S. stock market. For an energy infrastructure company with steady cash flows, that’s an impressive performance.

Despite global macroeconomic uncertainty, I remain extremely bullish on ENB. This is a company with rock-solid fundamentals and a unique position in the North American midstream industry. Furthermore, unlike oil producers whose profits are tied to the rise and fall of crude prices, ENB earns the bulk of its revenue from transporting and processing energy. And this is a setup that generates a highly stable cash flow. Another reason for my bullish stance is the current AI revolution. There’s no denying that running data centers sucks up an enormous amount of power, much of which is still sourced from natural-gas-fired plants. As ENB owns and operates a massive natural gas pipeline, it is in the perfect position to benefit from the AI wave. Finally, ENB’s valuation remains highly attractive. Add in the steady cash flow, solid dividends, and AI-driven demand, I believe this company is a compelling investment opportunity. All in all, I am reiterating my "Strong Buy" rating.

Recent developments

ENB’s latest quarterly earnings, released two months ago, were excellent. A quick recap: The company delivered a double beat against Wall Street’s expectations. And these weren’t marginal wins either. There were some quite surprising positives, reflecting a strong operation.

Both revenue and EPS demonstrated solid year-over-year growth, fueled mainly by Gas Transmission, which is the core of ENB’s business model, and this business's growth has bright future as it is closely linked to secular tailwinds that I will discuss below.

Thanks to solid and stable profitability, ENB’s free cash flow remains strong and supports the company’s financial health. The balance sheet is stellar, with a total debt of $74 billion, significantly lower than the $108 billion market cap. While you could argue that the debt looks a little high compared to the sub-$1-billion cash balance as of the latest reportable date, it is actually a fairly standard figure for a capital-intensive business like oil and gas midstream. After all, this is a business that requires heavy upfront investment in infrastructure, an investment that goes on to yield steady cash flows for decades to come. So the fact that total debt is notably lower than the market cap indicates that Enbridge’s financial flexibility for new potential projects is actually quite strong.

The strength of the balance sheet gives ENB the financial flexibility to keep expanding into new projects, while also supporting its dividend. I therefore believe ENB’s 5.44% forward dividend yield is safe. Moreover, during the latest earnings call, the management reaffirmed their 2025 full-year and multi-year outlooks, showing a strong confidence in sustaining and growing payouts.

For some investors, a 5.5% dividend yield might not seem that appealing when U.S. rates are still relatively high. For investors looking for a safer option, I recommend taking a look at the Series 1 Cumulative Preferred Shares (OTC:EBBGF), currently offering a 6.7% forward yield. Dividends of preferred shares have priority over common stock dividends, meaning higher safety. Personally, I would rather stick to the common shares due to my age and risk appetite, but I believe the latter option would be interesting for more risk-averse investors.

The crucial thing to remember here is that ENB is not only distributing dividends, it’s also heavily investing in growth. And AI is where it’s at. I’d like to highlight several new projects and tailwinds that not only show just how aggressively this company is preparing for an AI-driven future, but also what a pivotal role it will play in the AI’s overall rollout.

I’ll start with ENB’s recent announcement to go ahead with two U.S. transmission projects as part of its $23 billion capital program. These expansions are tied to the growing demand from data centers and LNG exports. The Northeast expansions will supply gas to power-thirsty data centers, while the Permian-to-Gulf Coast pipeline will feed the LNG exports that are increasingly fueling the global AI infrastructure. These assets will lock in contracted revenue for decades to come, right at a time when AI energy demand is about to explode.

A major push for ENB is the sheer volume of gas required to run AI infrastructure. According to EQT’s CEO, AI is expected to drive a staggering 20-40% increase in U.S. natural gas demand. This is when ENB’s extensive pipeline network will really come into its own, providing a critical part of the infrastructure required as it transports gas from production areas to the energy-thirsty data centers and LNG export facilities. In short, whether it’s data centers in Virginia or LNG exports in Louisiana. ENB is the crucial middleman.

Citi adds weight to this development by predicting that hyperscale cloud providers like Amazon, Microsoft, and Google will spend $2.8 trillion by 2029 on building AI data centers. That’s a mind-blowing number. As per above, these are projects that demand a vast amount of power, and mainly natural gas. And once again ENB’s network is right in the heart of the supply chain.

Politics may add another tailwind, especially if President Trump’s recent comments are anything to go by. His stance reflects his general skepticism toward renewables and a preference for fossil fuels. In this context, I predict U.S. policy will lean towards supporting such energy sources over wind and solar power, meaning that natural gas pipelines such as those owned by ENB will become even more strategically important.

The above bullish factors highlight one simple point: the AI boom is also an energy transportation boom, where natural gas is the fuel that can make AI happen. And ENB is in the perfect position to capture a large share of the infrastructure market. As a result, investor sentiment is also improving, backed by the share price momentum. While the stock has underperformed in the broader market for much of this year, the momentum has definitely turned.

Finally, seasonality is likely to add an end-of-year boost. As we can see below, ENB historically performs well in the last two months of the year. Taking into account the aforementioned bullish conditions, I don’t believe 2025 will be any different.

Valuation update

If we look at the valuation, ENB actually grades quite low across standard ratios compared to the sector median. However, I don’t find these comparisons particularly meaningful. Here’s why: ENB is unique with an unrivaled and extensive presence across North America. So instead of comparing sector medians, I prefer to study historical averages. And from this perspective, ENB’s valuation looks attractive. The company is not trading significantly above its fundamentals, which is to be expected as it is not a growth stock. It’s an infrastructure utility with a reliable cash flow.

Looking ahead, Wall Street analysts expect the forward P/E ratio to compress, supporting my belief that ENB is attractively valued. In short, investors are paying for a pipeline of contracted projects that will support higher profits in the future.

Risks update

Several risks stand out and are worth mentioning. Firstly, as a large company transporting hydrocarbons, ENB comes under major ongoing environmental, social, and governance scrutiny. New and constantly changing regulations could impose stricter environmental standards, which would mean the company would have to increase spending on factors such as safety and maintenance, incurring financial costs and losing time. A delay in just one pipeline could cost the company millions in revenue.

Another risk is the economy itself. ENB will not be immune to the U.S. economic slowdown. While the company is less sensitive to oil and gas prices than producers, it is still dependent on volumes, and these could weaken significantly in the wake of slowing energy usage in case of the broader macroeconomic cyclical downturn.

Bottom line

To conclude, ENB remains a "Strong Buy". Financially, ENB continues to deliver on all fronts with a stable cash flow, solid balance sheet, and attractive dividends. This is bolstered by both long-term growth opportunities and political tailwinds. With the company’s heavy investment into gas transmission projects, it is not only poised to capitalize on the AI boom but also to become a pivotal part of its infrastructure. The result is an attractive valuation, especially from a historical perspective, with strong dividend safety.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ChargePoint: Potential Value Trap - Structural/Growth/Bottom-Line Headwinds Remain
data.description:
CHPT's Headwinds Mount As The Stock Enters Oversold Territories

I previously covered ChargePoint Holdings (NYSE:CHPT) in April 2024, discussing how the stock might remain volatile, attributed to the dangerous combination of being highly shorted and the penny stock prices.

Despite the growing annualized subscription revenues and the growing stickiness of its platform, the company had remained unprofitable then, worsened by the plateauing first round of EV adoption, the record high borrowing costs, and the delayed launch of affordable mass-market EVs.

CHPT 1Y Stock Price

Since that reiterated Hold rating, CHPT has indeed retraced by another -59.5% compared to the wider market at +33.4%, while it notably underperforms its EV charging peers by large degrees.

Much of its headwinds are attributed to the new US administration's reversal in EV policies, as observed in the One Big Beautiful Bill halting these tax credits:

  • the 30D New Clean Vehicle Credit ($7.5K EV tax credit for consumers) from September 30, 2025 onwards,
  • the 30C Alternative Fuel Vehicle Refueling Property Credit (electric vehicle charging equipment) from June 30, 2026 onwards, and
  • the 45W Commercial Clean Vehicle Credit ($40K EV tax credit for fleet operators) from September 30, 2025 onwards,

with it apparent that the EV sector is likely to face a drastically different supply and demand pattern, assuming that spending trends and investment cycles are impacted by the changing regulatory environment.

This is especially since CHPT has been reporting a painfully decelerating top-line growth profile after the explosive double-digit growth observed in FY2022 and FY2023, worsened by its inherent lack of profitability thus far.

The management has further fed the pessimism with a mixed FQ3'26 revenue guidance of $95M at the midpoint (-4% QoQ/-12.4% YoY) and a pushed out EBITDA breakeven target beyond FY2026, with it hinting at further growth headwinds and elevated cash burn cadence ahead.

With a deteriorating balance sheet at a growing net debt position of -$115.29M in FQ2'26 (-3% QoQ/-171.8% YoY) and a negative free cash flow generation of -$41.47M in H1'26 (+65.7% YoY), I believe that CHPT may further dilute its existing shareholders moving forward.

This is based on the last share count of 23.19M (+8.5% YoY), the September 2025 $400M mixed shelf offering, and the elevated annualized stock based compensation of $72.14M in H1'26 (-10.6% YoY), despite the recently completed reverse stock split at a ratio of 1-for-20 in July 2025.

This is especially since CHPT continues to rely on Networked Charging Systems sales to drive its top-line growth, with the ChargePoint as a Service subscription revenues remaining a small portion of its overall FQ2'26 revenues at $39.89M (+4.9% QoQ/+10.2% YoY) at a ratio of 40.4% (+1.4 points QoQ/+7.1 YoY).

Pending the recovery of the North American region attributed to the mixed regulatory policy, there appear to be minimal growth prospects in Europe as well, as observed in the latter's YoY revenue decline in FQ2'26.

At a time when its direct competitor, EVgo (EVGO), is consistently reporting a double-digit growth profile, while raising their FY2025 guidance, it is unsurprising that CHPT's underperformance has been translated to the stock's ongoing meltdown indeed.

It cannot be helped that 57% of EVGO's chargers are at 350 kilowatt hours (fast chargers), compared to CHPT's at 10.1% at DC fast charging, as drivers increasingly demand fast charging capability, with over 60% of surveyed drivers "wanting charging times of 30 minutes or less."

These reasons may also be why CHPT has already partnered with Eaton to develop "ultrafast charging architecture with end-to-end power infrastructure for public charging and fleets. ChargePoint Express Grid, powered by Eaton, is a vehicle-to-everything (V2X) capable solution that delivers up to 600kW of power for passenger EVs and can provide megawatt charging for heavy-duty commercial applications."

The ultrafast charging technology may very well drive accelerated growth for the market leader indeed, especially since it "can be deployed with 30% lower capex in a 30% smaller footprint while delivering up to a 30% reduction in ongoing operational costs, compared to other available solutions."

With CHPT already operationalizing their go-to-market relationship while recording the first revenue deals, readers may expect the company to report renewed growth opportunities beyond the trough FY2025 performance indeed.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

Otherwise, pending a promising early result from the partnership in FQ3'26, I can understand why the consensus has further lowered their forward estimates, with CHPT expected to report a decelerating top-line growth profile at a CAGR of +10.5% through FY2028, against the +21% observed a year ago.

Given the H1'26 revenue underperformance at -8.9% YoY, these numbers do not appear to be overly pessimistic as well, with it remaining to be seen when the company may eventually achieve operating scale and report break-even, much less adj EBITDA profitability, based on the FQ2'26 adj EBITDA performance at -$22.1M (+3% QoQ/+35.1% YoY).

CHPT Valuations

As a result of its mixed growth prospects and the ongoing cash burn, I can understand why the market has consistently discounted CHPT's FWD EV/Sales valuations to 0.98x, down from the 1Y mean of 1.06x and the 5Y mean of 8.82x.

When comparing to the sector FWD EV/Sales median valuations of 2.14x and the EV charging peers, including:

  • EVGO at 3.67x,
  • Blink Charging Co. (BLNK) at 1.56x,
  • Enphase (ENPH) at 3.11x, and
  • Tesla (TSLA) at 16.17x,

it goes without saying that CHPT has entered oversold territories.

Assuming that the Eaton partnership delivers renewed growth from FY2027 onwards, as observed in the consensus forward estimates above, if not from H2'26, I believe that the bulls may support its upcoming recovery while re-rating the stock closer to its EV charging peer EV/Sales valuation mean of ~2.33x.

The same has been observed in CHPT's relatively compelling estimated FY2028 EV/Sales valuations of 0.70x, based on the Enterprise Value of $397.74M at the time of writing and the consensus FY2028 (CY2027) sales estimates of $563.18M.

When compared to EVGO's estimated FY2027 (CY2027) EV/Sales valuations of 2.23x, based on the Enterprise Value of $1.33B at the time of writing and the consensus FY2027 sales estimates of $594.56M, it is apparent that CHPT's discounted valuations present a contrarian buy opportunity for the brave.

So, Is CHPT Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

CHPT 2Y Stock Price

For now, CHPT's bottom has seemingly materialized in the $8.50s range by July 2025, with the stock also climbing by +32.3% since then to retest the prior resistance levels of $11s.

CHPT's Mixed Technical Indicators

The bullish support has also been observed in the growing trading volumes since early 2025, with the reasonable Relative Strength Index [RSI] implying further upward momentum despite the double-digit recovery.

Even so, it goes without saying that risks remain in CHPT's investment thesis, as observed in the declining sales cadence, the ongoing cash burn, and the deteriorating balance sheet, worsened by the still elevated short interest ratio of 14.9%.

While the stock may appear to be cheap here, it also presents a potential value trap pending the renewed growth prospects arising from the Eaton partnership.

As a result of the mixed investment story and the painful -98.7% meltdown from the December 2020 heights, I prefer to miss the first leg up and wait for a clearer growth tailwind to emerge before changing my views.

CHPT remains a hold here.
